analogy: Think of "Introducing Indian Society" through the chapter's own opening images: Indian Society 2 In one important sense, Sociology is unlike any other subject that you may have studied. It is a subject in which no one starts from zero – everyone already knows something about society. Other subjects are learnt because they are taught (at... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Introducing Indian Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that indian Society 2 In one important sense, Sociology is unlike any other subject that you may have studied.

The text explains that it is a subject in which no one starts from zero – everyone already knows something about society.

The text explains that other subjects are learnt because they are taught (at school, at home, or elsewhere); but much of our knowledge about society is acquired without explicit teaching.

The text explains that because it is such an integral part of the process of growing up, knowledge about society seems to be acquired “naturally” or “automatically”.

The text explains that no child is expected to already know something about History, Geography, Psychology or Economics when they come to school.

The text explains that but even a six year old already knows something about society and social relationships.

The text explains that it is all the more true then, that, as young eighteen year old adults, you know a lot about the society you live in without ever having studied it.

The text explains that this prior knowledge or familiarity with society is both an advantage and a disadvantage for sociology, the discipline that studies society.

The text explains that the advantage is that students are generally not afraid of Sociology – they feel that it can’t be a very hard subject to learn.

The text explains that the disadvantage is that this prior knowledge can be a problem – in order to learn Sociology, we need to “unlearn” what we already know about society.

The text explains that in fact, the initial stage of learning Sociology consists mainly of such unlearning.

The text explains that this is necessary because our prior knowledge about society – our common sense – is acquired from a particular viewpoint.

The text explains that this is the viewpoint of the social group and the social environment that we are socialised into.

The text explains that our social context shapes our opinions, beliefs and expectations about society and social relations.

The text explains that these beliefs are not necessarily wrong, though they can be.

The text explains that the word partial is being used here in two different senses – incomplete (the opposite of whole), and biased (the opposite of impartial).

The text explains that this is the ability to reflect upon yourself, to turn back your gaze (which is usually directed outward) back towards yourself.

The text explains that other aspects of your individual identity may be sources of pride, tension, confidence or embarrassment in different ways.

The text explains that finally, the family as an institution has also been subjected to tremendous pressure in these times of rapid and intense social change.

The text explains that how much of an impact has the women’s movement had on the social institutions that have oppressed women?

The text explains that chapter 6 deals with the difficult challenges posed by the immense diversity of Indian society.

The text explains that this chapter invites us to step outside our normal, comfortable ways of thinking.

The text explains that the familiar cliches and slogans about India being a land of unity in diversity have a hard and complex side to them.

The text explains that despite all the failures and inadequacies, India has not done too badly on this front.

The text explains that what have been our strengths and our weaknesses?

The text explains that how may young adults face issues like communal conflict, regional or linguistic chauvinism, and casteism without either wishing them away or being overwhelmed by them?

The text explains that why is it important for our collective future as a nation that every minority in India not feel that it is insecure or at risk?

The text explains that finally, in Chapter 7, some suggestions are provided for you and your teachers to think about the practical component of your course.

The text explains that this can be quite interesting and enjoyable, as you will discover.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Indian Society, Chapter 1: Introducing Indian Society

cases.name: Indian Society
cases.point: Indian Society 2 In one important sense, Sociology is unlike any other subject that you may have studied. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: People
cases.point: People your age or younger account for about forty per cent of India’s population. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: You
cases.point: You might belong to a particular regional or linguistic community, such as a Gujarati speaker from Gujarat or a Telugu speaker from Andhra Pradesh. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: Introducing Indian Society
cases.point: Introducing Indian Society 3 these identities would locate you on a social map, and among a web of social relationships. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 2)

cases.name: As
cases.point: As C.Wright Mills, a well-known American sociologist has written, sociology can help you to map the links and connections between “personal troubles” and “social issues”. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: How
schemes.point: How has this institution been changing over the centuries, and what does caste really mean today? (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: Finally
schemes.point: Finally, the family as an institution has also been subjected to tremendous pressure in these times of rapid and intense social change. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: Chapter
schemes.point: Chapter 4 explores the socio-cultural dimensions of the market as a powerful institution that has been the vehicle of change throughout world history. (NCERT Class 12 Indian Society Ch1 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.2 A Preview of this B ook and Introducing Indian Society as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Introducing Indian Society; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Introducing Indian Society through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.2 A Preview of this B ook and Introducing Indian Society to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
